Comply with JMC No. 2013-01 in informing the DILG Regional Office in writing should changes in the DILG-approved GPB occur and/or if there is a need to implement additional PPAs relevant to current gender issues or GAD-related undertakings.

Judiciously plan the prioritization of projects by considering the following:

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management take the following courses of action:
Require the OIC-Municipal Accountant to submit immediately the corrected BRS as of December 31, 2019, enforce Section 122 of Presidential Decree (P.D.) No. 1445 on suspension of payment of salaries of officials required to submit documents and reports to the COA;
Identify to which the three accounts mentioned above relate and determine the propriety of its treatment in the FS; and
Strictly adhere with the provisions of COA Circular No. 96-011 on the timely and regular submission of monthly BRS.

We recommended that Management:
Verify the amounts mentioned above and ensure that appropriate adjusting entry(ies) is/are made in order to correct the affected account;
Ensure compliance with relevant provisions of pertinent PPSAS with regard to timely and appropriate recognition of interests earned from Time Deposit accounts; and
Evaluate the substance or nature of the Municipality’s investment in HYSA with LBP. Based from such evaluation, if warranted, effect the necessary reclassification of such account from Cash in Bank-Local Currency, Time Deposit to the appropriate asset account.

We recommended that Management take the following courses of action:
Require the Inventory Committee led by the GSO to conduct physical inventory taking of all the Agency’s assets, stating therein, among others, the property code, nature of the asset, amount, department, and date of purchase and other necessary information needed for the preparation of the RPCPPE;
Submit the properly accomplished RPCPPE as prescribed by existing regulations to the accounting department and the COA; and
Require the accounting department to reconcile the physical inventory with the PPE per books and adjust the records accordingly. Also, maintain corresponding PPELCs for each property in accordance with pertinent provisions of MNGAS.

We recommended that Management take the following courses of action:
Determine the total accumulated depreciation and make necessary adjustments to reflect the accurate balances of the PPE Accounts in the books; and
Ensure that depreciation expenses are recognized for each depreciable PPE every year.

We recommended that Management should provide necessary appropriation to facilitate the acquisition of land titles of lots of the Municipality to safeguard real properties and to document ownership of the property and in compliance with Section 449 of GAAM, Volume I. Also, reconcile the recorded land in the books with the actual physical inventory of land to avoid overstating or possible understating of the reported asset of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management take the following courses of action;
Require the Inventory Committee  to conduct physical inventory taking of Supplies Inventory semi-annually as required under the existing rules and regulations; and
Reconcile the Inventory Accounts per books with the actual supplies inventory at hand; and
Present fairly the Inventory Accounts in the FS for the next succeeding year.

We recommended that Management take the following courses of action:
Ensure continued prompt remittance to the  GSIS of the monthly contributions (Government and Personal Share), loan amortizations, and employee compensation premium;
Through the AAO, timely and regularly manifest in writing to the GSIS by means of coordinating monthly thru e-mail with the said agency the need to resolve the recurring concern where its electronic bill is not updated despite LGU’s diligence in the timely updating of employee service records; and
Warrant the timely recording in the accounting system of amounts withheld and remitted to the GSIS; and
Ensure compliance with Sections 5 and 6 (b) of R.A.  No. 8291.

The accounts SET Receivables and Deferred SET were inadvertently interchanged as explained by the Management.
The debit to SET Receivables increased the account which should have been decreased as a result of collecting a receivable, and the credit to SET also increased the balance of the account instead of decreasing it as a result of the realization of deferred revenue.
The flawed entry was used from June 2016 to date even though this has been brought to the attention of the Management since CY 2017 audit.
As a result, the SET Receivables and Deferred SET Accounts are both overstated by ₱2.815 million in CY 2019.

We recommended that Management take the following courses of action:
Judiciously plan the prioritization of projects by considering the following:
Ensure that the purpose and intention of the PPAs to be appropriated with funds is correctly expressed in its title or description, thus, avoiding undue delay in the implementation;
Secure the commitment of the beneficiary Barangays in the implementation of the project, thus, funds are not tied up to projects which will not eventually implemented: and
Ensure effective utilization of the 20 per cent by limiting the prioritization of projects as to its necessity and urgency, thus, projects are funded adequately and implemented timely.
When warranted, consider reinforcing the manpower in the MEO by hiring additional engineers for the timely preparation of POWs, plans and drawings for the timely implementation of the PPAs;
Enhance the utilization of the 20 per cent of the IRA as prescribed in DBM-DILG JMC No. 2017-01; and
Ensure that at the end of the year, claim of Contractor arising from progress billing or final payment, provided it’s supported with complete required documentation and has followed the prescribed accounting procedures, must be obligated. Suppose payment cannot be made, be recognized as an accrued obligation payable at the earliest time possible. This measure reasonably bridges the gap between physical and financial utilization.

Require the BAC to ensure enforcement of the submission of CSHP for each specific LGU infrastructure projects; and
Ensure that CSHPs submitted by the contractors are in accordance with existing laws, rules and regulations, and other issuances by the DOLE. Primarily, the CSHP must be suitable and specific to the construction project.

Ensure compliance with the requirement on posting of the award of procurement opportunities under competitive bidding and SVP as mandated by the pertinent provisions of 2016 R-IRR of R.A. No. 9184.

30
Property and equipment of the LGU were not labeled with property stickers/

management of PPE accounts of the LGU. Thus, it contributes difficulty in identifying and
tracking the whereabouts of properties and the properties of the LGU are prone to risk of

) Unserviceable Properties

Section 4(p), Chapter 2, Volume 1 of the MNGAS provides, that, “assets declared
by proper authorities as obsolete and unserviceable, including assets of the agency no
longer used, shall be reclassified to Other Assets account for the corresponding inventory
and property, plant and equipment accounts.”

The municipality carries in its books numerous unserviceable PPEs which
overstate the asset of the municipality.

Further delay in the disposal of these PPEs and non-reclassification of the
unserviceable properties cause the PPE accounts to remain overstated as of December 31,
2016; the unserviceable properties, like vehicles and heavy equipment, continues to
deteriorate, thereby, reducing salvage value; and these properties take up a lot of storage
space and incur maintenance cost which could have been avoided.

We recommended that Management should require the GSO to fast track the

disposal of the unserviceable property. After which, the disposed assets should be
taken down from the books.

d) Land and Land Improvements
The validity and propriety of the Land and Land Improvements could not be
ascertained due to the absence of documents showing its legal ownership such as Title of

Property, Deed of Sale or Deed of Donation, and Tax Declaration that would show proof
that the property ownership belongs to the agency.

31
Section 449 of the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual (GAAM)
Volume I provides that “Land purchased by agencies of the government shall be evidenced
by a Torrens Title drawn in the name of the Republic of the Philippines, or such other
document satisfactory to the President of the Philippines that the title is vested in the
government.”

Ownership to land and land rights are established on the basis of genuine
Certificate of Titles in the Register of Deeds, free from any liens and encumbrances.

Verification of records of disclosed that lands where permanent buildings and
structures of the municipalities were erected totaling 19,181,652.93 were not covered by
land titles in the name of the agency.

Without these documents the Municipality’s legal claim over these lands are weak
and may pose risks such loss of the land, loss of the building and structures erected or
incurring extra expenditures for the acquisition of the same.

We recommended that Management should provide necessary appropriation
to facilitate the acquisition of land titles of lots of the Municipality to safeguard real
properties and to document ownership of the property and in compliance with section
449 of GAAM, Volume I. Also, reconcile the recorded land in the books with the
actual physical inventory of land to avoid overstating or possible understating of the
reported asset of the Municipality.

o Exercise economy and efficiency in estimating prices for construction materials for their infrastructure projects to avoid positive variance above the COA evaluated cost; and

o Be guided with the pertinent Department Orders of the Department of Public Works and Highways (DPWH) being the primary infra-agency of the government in the preparation of their estimates.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to submit immediately the required BRS as of December 31, 2014 otherwise suspend the payment of salaries due him until such time that these are completely submitted, in accordance with Section 122 of P.D. 1445.

Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain Subsidiary Ledgers for each depository account of Cash-in-Bank LCCA and LCSA to facilitate the preparation of BRS.
observations.managementAction: Bank Reconciliation Statement for CY 2016 has been submitted to the COA.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

o Strictly comply with the Framework for the Preparation and Presentation of FS and observe the Accounting Standards in the recording of expenses and of prior years’ adjustments;

o Instruct the accountant to effect adjusting entries to correct the misstatement in the interest income account in the amount of P338,316.65; and

o Instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain Subsidiary Ledger for Cash-in-bank /CTD/ High Yield deposits for purposes of reconciliation of bank and book records.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor directs the Municipal Treasurer to immediately remit all taxes withheld to the Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR) and ensure the timely remittance of all taxes witheld on the ensuing year.
observations.managementAction: Taxes from various sources are remitted promptly as discussed in Audit Observation No. 21 of this audit report.
observations.reason: Taxes from various sources are remitted promptly as discussed in Audit Observation No. 21 of this audit report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management should comply the requirements and intent of Section 21 of R.A. No. 8559 and the signed MOA in the implementation of Farm to Market Roads. Detailed estimates, engineering designs and program of works should be signed and sealed by a licensed Agricultural Engineer.

We recommended that Management should:

o Instruct the MDRRMO to prepare the LDRRMFIP and the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF (Annex A and B of COA Circ. 2012-002) in the prescribed format, identifying therein the QRF, Mitigation Fund and fund sources from Current Appropriation, Continuing Appropriation, and Prior Years Appropriations to facilitate the presentation of the MDRRMEF; and

o Instruct the Municipal Accountant to transfer the unexpended balances of the QRF and DRRM-MOOE from the General Fund to TF books at the end of the year and effect the following entries:

General Fund Books

Subsidy to other Funds(877)
Cash (111) 

Trust Fund Books
Cash (111)

Trust Liability - DRRMF(438)
(with subsidiary for the source and year).

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management should:

o Comply strictly COA Circular No.97-002 and RMC Circular No. 23-2007;

o Stop the practice of paying gasoline and fuel consumption through cash advances and reimbursements and follow the procurement process required in R.A. No. 9184; and

o Refund the amount of P63,628.45 to the government coffer the disallowable amount per audit.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management should require the Accounting Office to thoroughly review DVs and make sure that these are supported with complete documentation using as guide the Revised Documentary Requirements for Common Government Transactions prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001 and R.A. No. 9184 before submitting the DVs for approval.
observations.managementAction: Management ensure compliance with the recommendation.
observations.reason: Management did not strictly complied with COA Circular No. 2009-001 as discussed in Audit Observation No. 16 of this audit report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management stop immediately the practice of paying claims without adequate documentation to avoid suspensions and ultimately disallowances in audit. We likewise recommended that the Municipal Accountant adjust accordingly the erroneous accounting entries which affected the presentation of asset and expense accounts by £907,697.57.
observations.managementAction: Management substantially complied except for one DV of the Municipal Treasurer amounting $8,688.00. The Municipal Treasurer agreed to a salary deduction for the payment of the amount.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management intensify the collection of RP/SE Taxes and enforce collections from delinquent payors particularly the large ones so that these will eventually be appropriated for development projects of the community.
observations.managementAction:
Sta. Elena, Capalonga, Paracale, and Jose Panganiban all commented that the issue in the collection of RPT is impossible to address with the current system. The Management coordinated with the Provincial Government and all the LGUs. Also, compliance with R.A. No. 7160 and pertinent revenue codes should be strictly enforced and followed by the LGUs of Camarines Norte.

Usual problems include non-payment of current tax dues by land owners due to the idea of amnesty during elections and non-registry of transfer/subdividing of lands.
observations.reason: Even with the prior recommendations were addressed, the issue of rising RPT receivables remains.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing observations and in order that a timely and effective review could be performed, we recommended that management undergo the following courses of action:

o Ensure that the POs and contracts are submitted within five days from issuance thereof pursuant to COA Circular No. 76-34 dated July 15, 1976 and subsequent related issuance;

o See to it that all data and information required under COA Circular No. 96-010 dated August 15, 1996 are incorporated in the purchase orders; and

o Ensure that purchase orders and contracts and each of all the documents forming part thereof by reference or incorporation, as enumerated in COA Circular No. 76-34 and COA Circular No. 91-704 dated February 27, 1991 are furnished to the auditor to facilitate legal, auditorial and technical review.
observations.managementAction: Management ensured compliance with the recommendation.
observations.reason: Details of non-implementation are discussed in Audit Observation No. 16 of this audit report.
